title: Why the return to feeling includes feelings you do not want
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The promise that healing will feel good is a lie

I believed it for years. The wellness industry sold me a story that if I just did the right yoga, drank the right tea, said the right affirmations, I would float into some kind of permanent peace. A state where my body felt calm, my mind felt clear, and my heart felt open. A state where I was finally healed.

Here's the thing. That state does not exist. Not for anyone with a real history of trauma. Not for anyone whose nervous system learned to survive by disconnecting from feeling altogether.

The return to feeling is not a return to peace. It is a return to everything you spent decades learning not to feel. And some of those feelings? They are genuinely awful. They are the feelings you locked in a basement and threw away the key. They are the feelings that made you want to disappear as a child. They are the feelings that convinced you that feeling anything at all was too dangerous to survive.

I remember the first time I really felt my anger. Not the polite, intellectualized version of anger that I could talk about in therapy. The real thing. The kind that made my hands shake and my jaw clench and my throat close. I sat in my car in a parking lot and I thought, I cannot do this. I cannot feel this. This will kill me.

It didn't kill me. But it felt like it would. And that's the part nobody tells you about the return to feeling. It includes feelings you do not want. Feelings you actively hate. Feelings that make you question whether you were better off numb.

You were not better off numb. But I know it doesn't feel that way when the rage or the grief or the shame starts moving through your body for the first time in twenty years.

Your nervous system learned to hide from feeling

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, talks about how our nervous system has a hierarchy of responses. When we feel safe, we can connect, we can play, we can rest. When we feel threatened, we mobilize. We fight or we flee. And when neither of those is possible, when the threat is too big or too constant or too inescapable, we shut down. We dissociate. We go numb.

That numbness is not a failure. It is a brilliant survival strategy. It is your nervous system saying, I cannot process this experience right now without being destroyed. So I will put it in a box. I will seal the box. I will bury the box somewhere deep. And I will forget the box exists.

But here's what happens when you start to heal. You start to feel safe enough to open the box. And inside that box is not a neatly organized collection of memories you can process one at a time. Inside that box is a writhing mass of every feeling you ever refused to feel. Every moment of terror. Every wave of grief. Every shred of shame. Every drop of rage.

It comes out messy. It comes out all at once. It comes out in your body before your mind can make sense of it. You might wake up with your heart pounding and no idea why. You might feel a wave of sadness hit you in the grocery store for no apparent reason. You might snap at someone you love because your nervous system is screaming and you don't know how to make it stop.

This is not regression. This is not you getting worse. This is the return. And it is messy and painful and confusing and you will hate parts of it.

Peter Levine, who created somatic experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Not in the story you tell about what happened, but in the sensations that never got to complete their cycle. The tension that never released. The collapse that never got to reorganize into standing. The breath that never got to finish.

When you start to feel again, you are not just feeling emotions. You are feeling the unfinished movements of your body. The clench that has been held for thirty years. The held breath that has been waiting to exhale since you were five years old. A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link), which explains this process in a way that actually makes sense for real human bodies.

The feelings you least want to feel are the ones that need to come home

I have a theory about the feelings we resist most. They are not the most dangerous feelings. They are the most exiled feelings. They are the parts of us that got told, directly or indirectly, that they were not allowed to exist.

Maybe you were told that anger was bad. That good girls don't get mad. That you should be grateful, not furious. So your anger went underground. It turned into resentment, into passive aggression, into chronic tension in your shoulders and jaw. It turned into migraines. It turned into depression. It turned into a thousand symptoms that nobody connected to the simple truth that you were never allowed to say no.

Maybe you were told that sadness was weak. That crying was for babies. That you needed to be strong for everyone else. So your sadness got locked away. It turned into a hollow feeling in your chest that you couldn't name. It turned into a need to stay busy constantly so you wouldn't have to sit still. It turned into a voice that told you that you were fine, you were fine, you were fine, even when you were clearly not fine.

Maybe you were told that joy was dangerous. That if you got too happy, something bad would happen. That it was safer to stay flat, stay neutral, stay invisible. So your joy got suppressed. It turned into a life that was functional but empty. It turned into a career that looked successful from the outside but felt like nothing on the inside. It turned into a relationship where you were present but not really there.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link), which directly addresses how our exiled feelings are actually trying to protect us. Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems, calls these parts "exiles" for a reason. They are the parts of us that got locked away because they were too painful to hold. And when they come back, they come back with all the intensity they had when they were first banished.

That intensity is overwhelming. It can feel like you are being possessed by a version of yourself that you do not recognize. A version that is seven years old and sobbing. A version that is fifteen and screaming. A version that is twenty-three and shaking with terror.

Stay with me here. That version of you is not dangerous. That version of you is just very, very young and very, very hurt. And that version of you has been waiting a very long time to be seen.

Your body will tell you the truth whether you want to hear it or not

The return to feeling is not a mental exercise. You cannot think your way through it. You cannot journal your way out of it. You cannot talk about it enough to make it go away. The return to feeling happens in your body, and your body does not care about your timeline or your comfort level or your carefully constructed narrative about how healed you are.

Your body will wake you up at 3 AM with a panic attack because it finally feels safe enough to release the terror it has been holding for years. Your body will make you cry in the middle of a work meeting because a colleague said something that reminded you, somewhere deep, of a voice you have not heard in decades. Your body will make you want to crawl out of your skin because it is finally thawing out from the freeze response that kept you alive but kept you numb.

I have sat with clients who looked at me with absolute desperation and said, "I was fine before I started this work. I was managing. I was functional. Why would I choose to feel this?"

And I get it. I really do. Because there is a part of me that still wants to go back to the numbness sometimes. The numbness was predictable. The numbness was safe. The numbness did not make me sob in the shower or rage at my partner or feel like I was falling apart at the seams.

But the numbness was also not living. It was surviving. It was existing in a state of suspended animation, waiting for something that never came. It was being a ghost in my own life.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link), where Judith Herman explains that recovery happens in stages and that the return to feeling is not linear. It is not a straight line from numb to healed. It is a spiral. You will circle back to feelings you thought you had processed. You will be surprised by grief that you thought was finished. You will find anger where you thought there was only sadness.

This is not failure. This is the nature of the work.

You can survive feeling what you have been avoiding

I want to tell you something that might be hard to hear. The reason you have been avoiding these feelings is not because they are too big for you. It is because you have been carrying them alone. And no feeling is too big to be held when it is held with compassion. No feeling is too overwhelming when it is met with presence instead of resistance.

The problem was never the feelings themselves. The problem was that you had to feel them in isolation. You had to feel them without anyone to witness them. You had to feel them while also trying to survive a childhood or a relationship or a system that was actively hurting you. Of course you had to lock them away. That was the smartest thing you could have done.

But you are not that child anymore. You are not in that situation anymore. You have resources now that you did not have then. You have a body that is stronger than you think. You have a nervous system that can learn to return to regulation after it gets dysregulated. You have the capacity to feel these feelings and survive them.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link), which offers exercises for exactly this kind of gradual, supported return to feeling. It is not a quick fix. Nothing about this work is quick. But it is a way to start building the capacity to feel without being destroyed by what you feel.

The return to feeling is not about feeling good. It is about feeling real. It is about coming back into your body after years of living in your head. It is about letting your heart break open so it can finally learn to beat fully. It is about sitting in the mess of being human without needing to clean it up or explain it away.

You will feel things you do not want to feel. You will feel rage that scares you. Grief that drowns you. Shame that makes you want to hide. Loneliness that feels like it will never end. And you will survive all of it. Not because you are strong, though you are. But because feeling is what bodies are designed to do. Feelings move through. They have a beginning, a middle, and an end. They are not permanent. They are not who you are. They are just weather passing through the of your body.

The return to feeling includes feelings you do not want. But it also includes feelings you have been starving for. It includes the relief of finally being able to cry. The liberation of finally being able to say no. The sweetness of joy that is not afraid to exist. The peace that comes not from avoiding pain, but from knowing you can survive it.

You do not have to want this process. You do not have to like it. You just have to keep going. One feeling at a time. One breath at a time. One moment of yourself at a time.

That is the return. And it is worth it. Even when it hurts. Especially when it hurts.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
